skill.name: github-actions-docs
skill.description: Use when users ask how to write, explain, customize, migrate, secure, or troubleshoot GitHub Actions workflows, workflow syntax, triggers, matrices, runners, reusable workflows, artifacts, caching, secrets, OIDC, deployments, custom actions, or Actions Runner Controller, especially when they need official GitHub documentation, exact links, or docs-grounded YAML guidance.
skill.summary: Ground your GitHub Actions answers in official documentation with authoritative links and accurate guidance.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: All 93 static analysis findings are false positives from markdown code formatting and legitimate documentation URLs. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code. The backtick patterns detected are inline code formatting in markdown (e.g., `GITHUB_TOKEN`, `gh-fix-ci`), not shell execution. URLs are all official GitHub documentation links. No security risks present.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: 93 patterns detected by static analyzer are all false positives: 22 external_commands findings are markdown code formatting (backticks for inline code like `GITHUB_TOKEN`), 64 network findings are legitimate docs.github.com URLs, 4 env_access findings are documentation about GitHub tokens, 1 system reconnaissance and 1 weak crypto finding are scanner errors. This skill contains only markdown documentation with no executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed all patterns are markdown formatting and documentation URLs, not executable code or security risks

content.value_statement: Stop relying on memory for GitHub Actions questions. This skill grounds answers in official GitHub docs with authoritative links and accurate, up-to-date guidance.
actual_capabilities: Search and retrieve official GitHub Actions documentation for workflow syntax, triggers, and concepts
Provide accurate guidance on workflow authoring including jobs, matrices, variables, contexts, and expressions
Answer questions about GitHub-hosted runners, self-hosted runners, and Actions Runner Controller
Explain security best practices for secrets, GITHUB_TOKEN, OpenID Connect, and artifact attestations
Guide users through migrations from Jenkins, CircleCI, GitLab CI/CD, and other CI systems
Troubleshoot workflow issues with documentation-grounded answers and exact reference links
limitations: Only provides documentation and guidance - cannot directly modify or execute workflows in your repository
Documentation links may become outdated as GitHub updates their docs - verify critical information
Not intended for debugging specific failing workflow runs - use gh-fix-ci skill for CI failure triage
Does not cover CodeQL-specific configuration or Dependabot setup - use dedicated skills for those topics

use_cases.title: Learn GitHub Actions Basics
use_cases.description: New to GitHub Actions and need foundational knowledge about workflows, triggers, and basic syntax

use_cases.title: Write Complex Workflow Logic
use_cases.description: Building advanced workflows with matrices, reusable workflows, environment-specific deployments, or custom actions

use_cases.title: Secure Workflows with OIDC
use_cases.description: Migrating from long-lived cloud credentials to OpenID Connect for secure deployments

prompt_templates.title: Get Started with GitHub Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I create a basic GitHub Actions workflow that runs tests on every push?

prompt_templates.title: Understand Workflow Syntax
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain GitHub Actions workflow syntax for job matrices, conditional execution, and passing data between jobs

prompt_templates.title: Implement Security Best Practices
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I use OpenID Connect to deploy to AWS without storing long-lived credentials?

prompt_templates.title: Migrate from Another CI System
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me migrate my CI pipeline from Jenkins to GitHub Actions

output_examples.input: How do I use environment variables in GitHub Actions?
output_examples.output: In GitHub Actions, you can use variables in workflows through several methods:1) Custom configuration variables at the repository, environment, or organization level 2) Default environment variables provided by GitHub 3) Variables defined in the workflow file using the `vars` context or the `env` key. According to GitHub documentation, you reference variables as $VARIABLE_NAME in shell scripts or ${{ vars.VARIABLE_NAME }} in expressions. See the Variables documentation for complete details on scoping and precedence.

output_examples.input: What are reusable workflows and when should I use them?
output_examples.output: Reusable workflows allow you to share workflow templates across multiple repositories, avoiding duplication. Use them when you have the same or similar workflow logic in multiple places. According to GitHub docs, you call a reusable workflow with the 'uses' keyword in a job, passing inputs and secrets as needed. This is different from composite actions, which are reusable steps within a workflow.

best_practices: Always verify the current documentation for critical workflows - GitHub updates their docs regularly
Use specific documentation links rather than the general docs landing page for faster navigation
Combine documentation review with testing in your repository to validate workflow behavior
Start with overview pages for concepts, then reference pages for syntax details
anti_patterns: Relying on memory for GitHub Actions guidance - documentation changes and practices evolve
Using generic CI/CD advice instead of GitHub-specific patterns for workflow authoring
Treating repo-specific CI debugging as documentation questions - use gh-fix-ci skill instead
Providing workflow examples without grounding them in official documentation

faq.question: What is the difference between this skill and gh-fix-ci?
faq.answer: This skill provides official GitHub Actions documentation for general questions about workflow syntax, features, and concepts. Use gh-fix-ci when you need to debug a specific failing workflow run in your repository.

faq.question: Does this skill write workflows for me?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides documentation-grounded guidance and examples based on official GitHub docs. It helps you understand how to write workflows but does not directly create or modify workflow files in your repository.

faq.question: Are the documentation links always current?
faq.answer: The skill references official GitHub documentation, but links may change over time. GitHub periodically updates and reorganizes their docs. Always verify critical information on the live docs.github.com site.

faq.question: Can I use this for CodeQL or Dependabot questions?
faq.answer: No. For CodeQL configuration or code scanning questions, use the codeql skill. For Dependabot setup and grouping, use the dependabot skill. This skill focuses on general GitHub Actions workflows and features.

faq.question: What types of deployment questions can this help with?
faq.answer: This skill provides documentation on GitHub Actions deployment concepts including environments, deployment protection rules, and deployment history. It also references cloud-specific deployment guides like AWS ECS and Azure App Service, but focuses on the GitHub Actions side of deployments.

faq.question: How does this skill help with migrations?
faq.answer: The skill provides access to GitHub's migration documentation including the migration hub overview and platform-specific guides for Jenkins, CircleCI, GitLab CI/CD, and others. It can explain GitHub Actions equivalents for concepts from other CI systems.
